title: The Sonnets XCII - But do thy worst to steal thyself away
content:
But do thy worst to steal thyself away,
    For term of life thou art assured mine;
    And life no longer than thy love will stay,
    For it depends upon that love of thine.
    Then need I not to fear the worst of wrongs,
    When in the least of them my life hath end.
    I see a better state to me belongs
    Than that which on thy humour doth depend:
    Thou canst not vex me with inconstant mind,
    Since that my life on thy revolt doth lie.
    O! what a happy title do I find,
    Happy to have thy love, happy to die!
    But what�s so blessed-fair that fears no blot?
    Thou mayst be false, and yet I know it not.
meaning:
"The Sonnets XCII - But do thy worst to steal thyself away" is a quintessential example of William Shakespeare's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
